The CUPPER DAQ: Engineering the Microvolt
A deep-dive into the analog front-end design of a 2-channel EMG board built through recursive decomposition.

import EMGSignalVisualizer from '../../react/components/EMGSignalVisualizer';

![CUPPER DAQ Board](../../assets/blog/cupper_held.png)

## The Biological Challenge

Extracting surface electromyography (sEMG) signals is an exercise in extreme signal-to-noise ratios. A human forearm is a noisy antenna. When a muscle fiber fires, the resulting action potential propagates through thick layers of fat, interstitial fluid, and skin—acting as a massive low-pass filter and attenuator. By the time that signal reaches a pair of dry Ag/AgCl electrodes, its amplitude is measured in microvolts ($\mu V$).

But it's not just small; it's drowning. We are bathed in a sea of 50/60Hz electromagnetic interference from the very lights and wall outlets around us. We have motion artifacts from the electrode sliding against the skin during movement, which manifests as a massive, sweeping low-frequency baseline wander.

As part of **[Daidalonic](https://daidalonic.com/)**—a student-led group dedicated to developing biomedical devices—we needed a robust way to extract these buried intent signals in real-time for our prosthetic competition. This birthed **CUPPER**: a 2-channel EMG filtering PCB designed strictly through recursive decomposition. We refused to drop a "black box" biometric IC onto the board. If we didn't understand the discrete math of the signal, we didn't use it.

## The Signal Chain Architecture

To pull the signal from the noise, we designed a multi-stage analog front-end (AFE). Each stage in the CUPPER DAQ has a singular, mathematically precise role.

### 1. The Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)
The journey begins at the Instrumentation Amplifier. Its job is Common Mode Rejection (CMRR)—subtracting the noise present on both electrodes (like the 60Hz hum) and amplifying only the difference between them. Initially configured for a gain of $10 V/V$, we later boosted it to $17 V/V$.

### 2. The High-Pass Filter (HPF)
Next, the signal hits a $30 \text{Hz}$ active High-Pass Filter. This is critical. The baseline wander from movement artifacts sits heavily between $0.1 \text{Hz}$ and $20 \text{Hz}$. If we amplify the raw signal further without stripping this low-frequency drift, we risk saturating our op-amps and clipping the signal entirely. 

### 3. Isolation
Safety and SNR dictated a galvanic isolation stage. This protects the human from any fault currents back-propagating from the microcontroller, whilst simultaneously breaking ground loops that introduce massive noise.

### 4. The Post-Amp & Low-Pass Filter (LPF)
Once safely across the isolation barrier, we hit the signal with a massive $60 V/V$ post-amplification stage, bringing our total system gain to roughly $1020 V/V$ ($17 \times 60$). Immediately after, a $300 \text{Hz}$ Low-Pass filter strips out high-frequency thermal noise and serves as our anti-aliasing filter before digitization.

### 5. RMS-to-DC Conversion
Finally, the signal passes through a precision rectifier and integrator, effectively outputting the Root-Mean-Square (RMS) envelope of the EMG bursts rather than the raw 100Hz audio-like waveform.

![Oscilloscope Trace](../../assets/blog/cupper_oscilloscope_rms_lpf.jpg)
*Oscilloscope reading from a test session: The purple trace shows the high-frequency LPF output (muscle bursting), while the yellow trace is the clean, integrated RMS DC envelope ready for the ADC.*

Below is an interactive simulation of the CUPPER signal chain, demonstrating how the signal morphs as it passes through each stage.

<EMGSignalVisualizer client:only="react" />

## The Iteration: When the Math Fails

Our initial schematics specified the INA gain at $10 V/V$. On paper, this was elegantly conservative. Combined with the $60 V/V$ post-amp, a $600 V/V$ system gain should have scaled a typical $5 \text{mV}$ peak EMG signal to an easily digitized $3\text{V}$ swing.

But physics is unforgiving. During our first real-world dynamic tests, our ADC was starved. We were yielding only about $0.5 \text{V}$ swings during maximum voluntary contraction. 

Where did the math fail? **Skin-electrode impedance.**

Theoretical calculations often assume a pristine, low-impedance connection. In the chaotic environment of a competition, with dry electrodes and sweat, the impedance mismatch created an enormous voltage divider effect at the skin interface. Our actual $\mu V$ yield at the input terminals was a fraction of the physiological norm. 

We had to iterate. We ripped out the $10 V/V$ gain resistor and bumped the INA early-stage gain to $17 V/V$. By amplifying *harder* and *earlier* in the chain (before the noise floor was raised by subsequent active components), we hit our ADC's dynamic range perfectly without sacrificing our SNR.

## The Stability Choice: Realities of Competition

Why implement a hardware RMS converter? Many modern BCI approaches sample the raw, unfiltered waveform at $1000 \text{Hz}$ and perform digital filtering and feature extraction in software.

It came down to stability in high-stakes environments. A prosthetic competition is not a sterile lab. Microcontrollers fail, USB buses drop packets, and processing latency compounds. By handling the heavy lifting in hardware and outputting a slow, smooth, $0-3.3\text{V}$ analog DC envelope, we drastically reduced our embedded requirements. We could sample at a leisurely $50 \text{Hz}$, knowing the hardware had successfully integrated the energy of the muscle twitch. We sacrificed the nuanced frequency data of the raw waveform, but we gained absolute, unshakeable reliability when it mattered most.
Hodgkin–Huxley: From Ions to Action Potentials
A compact, intuitive walkthrough of the Hodgkin–Huxley conductance model and why it matters.

import LIFVisualization from '../../react/components/LIFVisualization';
import SNNVisualization from '../../react/components/SNNVisualization';
import HHVisualization from '../../react/components/HHVisualization';

## The Current Panorama

> We've all seen the clean spike trace, that sharp rise and fall that anyone who has performed electrophysiological recordings or in-silico modeling will recognise. But what makes that spike look the way it does? Why is it fast in one direction, slow in another, and impossible to half-build?

The Hodgkin–Huxley (HH) model is the answer that came from careful experiments on the squid giant axon in the 1940s and 50s. Hodgkin and Huxley didn't just measure spikes; they *dissected* the ionic machinery behind them and wrote it down as a set of coupled differential equations. The result is the canonical, conductance based neuron model used across neuroscience.


<SNNVisualization client:only="react" />

### A short history

Alan Hodgkin and Andrew Huxley used voltage clamps to isolate ionic currents and fit functions that described how ion channels open and close with voltage. The final product was a handful of ODEs with physiologically meaningful parameters. It explained excitability, thresholds, and the refractory period in a single unified framework.

![Squid Axon](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Giant_Axon_of_Squid_%2814356033761%29.jpg/250px-Giant_Axon_of_Squid_%2814356033761%29.jpg)
*The squid axon during one of the experiments*

## From membrane physics to math

At its heart the HH model treats the cell membrane as a capacitor with voltage dependent conductances for sodium and potassium plus a small leak conductance. The membrane equation is:

---
$$
C_m \frac{dV}{dt} = -\bar{g}_{\mathrm{Na}} m^3 h (V - E_{\mathrm{Na}}) - \bar{g}_{\mathrm{K}} n^4 (V - E_{\mathrm{K}}) - g_L (V - E_L) + I_{\mathrm{ext}}(t)
$$


Where:
- $C_m$ is membrane capacitance.
- $\bar{g}_{\mathrm{Na}}$, $\bar{g}_{\mathrm{K}}$ are the maximal conductances for sodium and potassium.
- $E_{\mathrm{Na}}$, $E_{\mathrm{K}}$, $E_L$ are the reversal (Nernst) potentials.
- $m$, $h$, $n$ are gating variables that control channel availability.
- $I_{\mathrm{ext}}(t)$ is any externally injected current.
---

The unusual exponents ($m^3 h$, $n^4$) encode the *cooperative* opening of gates inside an ion channel: for the sodium channel, three activation gates (modeled by $m$) and one inactivation gate ($h$) must be in the permissive state for the channel to conduct.

## Gating dynamics: the other three equations

Each gating variable follows first order kinetics driven by voltage dependent rates:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x(V) (1 - x) - \beta_x(V) x, \qquad x \in \{m,n,h\}
$$

The $\alpha$ and $\beta$ functions were empirically fit by Hodgkin & Huxley. A commonly-used formulation (squid-axon parameters, $V$ in mV) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_n(V) &= 0.01\frac{V + 55}{1 - e^{-(V + 55)/10}}, &\beta_n(V) &= 0.125 e^{-(V + 65)/80},\\
\alpha_m(V) &= 0.1\frac{V + 40}{1 - e^{-(V + 40)/10}}, &\beta_m(V) &= 4 e^{-(V + 65)/18},\\
\alpha_h(V) &= 0.07 e^{-(V + 65)/20}, &\beta_h(V) &= \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(V + 35)/10}}.
\end{aligned}
$$

These expressions capture the fast voltage dependent opening of sodium activation ($m$), the slower inactivation ($h$), and the delayed potassium activation ($n$). The interplay of fast activation and slower opposing currents is what sculpts the action potential.

If you wish to simulate this yourself, the code is available in the GitHub Repo linked in the footer below.

## Intuition: the spike as a dynamic balance

Think of $m$ as an accelerator for sodium current: when $V$ becomes slightly depolarised, $m$ races up and briefly opens the sodium gates, producing a runaway inward current that causes the upstroke. Meanwhile, $h$ (the sodium inactivation) and $n$ (the potassium activation) move on slower clocks and ultimately shut the sodium current down while opening potassium to pull the membrane back down.

This cascade explains familiar features:
- **Threshold**: small perturbations decay, but once $m$ crosses a tipping point the positive feedback wins.
- **Refractory period**: primarily governed by the slow time constant of the $h$-gate (sodium inactivation); $h$'s slow recovery sets the absolute/relative refractory timing that prevents immediate re-excitation.
- **Spike shape and amplitude**: set by reversal potentials and maximal conductances.

To see these dynamics in action, below is a visualization. Notice how the membrane potential "escapes" into a full spike only when the input current pushes it past the critical excitation threshold.

<HHVisualization client:only="react" />

## Example: how this compares to LIF

The Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) model reduces all of the above to a single linear ODE with a threshold. It's cheap to simulate and useful for large networks, but it cannot reproduce many HH behaviors (spike adaptation, subthreshold resonance, complex excitability classes) without additional mechanisms.

Below is the simpler LIF simulation for intuition; imagine replacing the single threshold here with the HH machinery to reveal the full biophysical waveform.

<LIFVisualization client:only="react" />

## Why HH matters (and why it's expensive)

The HH model is valuable because its parameters have direct physiological meaning: you can measure $\bar{g}_{\mathrm{Na}}$ or $E_{\mathrm{K}}$ experimentally. That makes it a canonical bridge between experiment and computation.

The price we pay is complexity. Each HH neuron carries continuous state (four variables) and nonlinear functions (exponentials, divisions) that demand careful numerical integration. Simple explicit integrators like Forward Euler require very small timesteps for stability and can be inaccurate; higher-order explicit methods such as Runge–Kutta (RK4) improve accuracy at the cost of extra work per step. Implicit and semi-implicit schemes (for example Crank–Nicolson or backward-Euler variants) are often used to trade per-step cost for larger stable timesteps. These trade-offs in timestep, accuracy, and compute cost are a practical constraint for large-scale HH simulations.

## Reduced models and practical trade-offs

If you need spikes cheaply, LIF or Izhikevich-style (another simple neuronal model) models are excellent engineering compromises. If you need detailed ionic currents or drug-channel interactions, HH (or derivatives) are essential. The trick (as always) is matching model fidelity to the biological question and the computational budget.

## Final note, realism vs pragmatism

Hodgkin and Huxley's work is a masterclass in how to extract a mechanistic model from data: start from physics, measure carefully, fit functions, and let the equations explain the phenomena. Today we inherit that legacy: biophysical models teach us what mechanisms *could* be doing the computation, while reduced models show how to build scalable systems that capture the behaviour we actually care about.
The Architectural Wall: Why Spatial AI Fails at Temporal Tasks.
Understaning the shortcommings of traditional artificial neural networks and how spiking neural networks (SNNs) solve them

import SNNVisualization from '../../react/components/SNNVisualization';
import LIFVisualization from '../../react/components/LIFVisualization';

## The Current Panorama

We've all been there, reading an LLM's response wondering how it could utter such nonsense. New releases promise PhD-like reasoning; they dont. 
Moreover they require **massive amounts of energy**, water and custom-built datacenters to run. How is it that you perform better than them whilst running on just a coffee and a granola bar?


## The Biggest Misconception in AI: Neural Networks

The human brain operates on around **20 Watts** of power. By contrast, a data center running an LLM like ChatGPT 5.4 demands **Megawatts**. There must be must something going on if the so called **"neural" networks** which 
are *supposed* to replicate the brain's architecture need **orders of magnitude** more energy to run. 

As you might have already guessed, these neural networks don't really replicate the brain's structure in the way your thought.

If we make the analogy between the LLM's "neurons" with your biological neurons, the articial neurons are constantly processing information whilst your smart neurons process information sparsely, only when its needed. 
This is know as a neuron "spiking" or "firing".

<SNNVisualization client:only="react" />

Lets now see how this behaviour can be achieved.

### The Leaky Integrate-and-Fire Model


We've already mentioned how biological neurons spike sparsely. They spike (roughly) based on weather or not the neurons its synapticly connected to also spike. 
Turns out that a neuron can decide how much importance to give to the firing of each of its synaptic neurons. This is known as **synaptic weights**. This is exactly what neural networks have copied!
Not the neurons themselves as we will now see but rather the idea of having a bunch of entities related to eachother by a numeric weight determining its importance.

Achieving brain-like behaviour (and efficiency) is as "simple" as replacing traditional neurons with their weight tensor, bias and activation function with a tensor of (synaptic) weights and a simulation of biological neuron.
A spiking neural network (SNN) is simply a traditional neural network where the articial neurons have been replaced by neurons that replicate real neuronal spiking, hence "spiking".

Luckily, this model has been figured out for decades thanks to the work done by Hodgkin and Huxley. Through a collection of 4 linear ordinary differential equations (ODEs), the effect that input currents (other neurons outputs) have
on the spiking of a neuron is perfectly captured. Without going into too much detail the neuron has an internal "counter" called the **membrane potential**, once this potential reaches a certain threshold (depolarisation) the neuron spikes and resets (repolarisation). 
If you want to learn more about the Hodgkin-Huxley model you can read about it [here](/blog/hh-model). 

Turns out however that simulating the HH model is extremely expensive and completly unusable in practice. However we only truly care about the spiking resembling a real neuron, we don't care about the exact vaues of the potential. This is where 
the Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) model comes into play. It's the foundation of many SNN architectures as it elegantly mimics the membrane potential $V(t)$ charging over time of a biological neuron. The dynamics are governed instead by a **single** first-order differential equation:


---
$$
\tau_m \frac{dV}{dt} = -[V(t) - E_L] + R_m I(t)
$$

Where:
- $\tau_m$ is the membrane time constant.
- $E_L$ is the resting potential of the neuron.
- $R_m$ represents the membrane resistance.
- $I(t)$ is the incoming current from pre-synaptic neurons.

---


Below is a live visualization of the LIF ODE. You can play with the constant current $I(t)$ and see how it reacts.

Notice how if the current is sub-threshold ($I < 1.0$), the passive membrane decay outpaces the accumulation. The neuron remains in a resting state. Once the input current drives the potential to the voltage threshold, 
the neuron fires an action potential (spike), followed by a hyperpolarization reset and a brief refractory period. In fact, the $-[V(t) - E_L]$ acts as a low pass filter, crucial in DSP.

<LIFVisualization client:only="react" />



## Discrepancies

Consider a prosthetic hand. A traditional ANN processes every pixel or EMG channel at a fixed frequency (e.g., 60Hz), wasting cycles on 'silence.' 
An SNN-based controller only 'wakes up' when a sensor detects a change in pressure or a motor spike. 
This is the difference between a system that is Always-On and a system that is **Event Driven**. The difference between your brain and ANNs.

They however aren't perfect.

## Reality Check

If this is so fantastic why isn't it being implemented everywhere? Turns out that simulating even the single ODE is expensive over a whole network. Artificial neural networks have evolved to squeeze the maximum performance out of our computer architectures 
through the extensive use of the GPU's incredibly fast tensor operation capabilities. And are in fact great at certain tasks like numeric computations or image processing.

Standard GPU architectures suffer from the **Von Neumann bottleneck**: energy expensive transfer of data between memory and the processor. 
We're missing hardware capable of efficiently replicating these neuronal dynamics: when a current is ran through it, it will spike and behave exactly the simulation. 
This hardware known as **Neuromorphic** and implements **In-Memory** computing, where the 'synapse' (weight) and the 'neuron' (processor) are physically co-located which enables massively parallel, event-driven computation.
Neuromorphic hardware like Intel’s **Loihi** or IBM’s **NorthPole** is starting to be released but it's still far from being usable at scale. This area is in fact at the very forefront of research.


With this post I hope you have learned what **SNNs** are and are able to appreciate how **revolutionary** these can be in event driven environments. Which is why I'm so excited about them as they are the key to **low-latency**, 
**low-power medical implants** like **BCIs**.
